discussion_comments.comment_body: in which case o can be NULL?

In the current code it cannot. I prefer for this to stay to be in sync with other `get.*From.*Object` functions, but I can remove it if you see fit.
EDIT: my bad. will remove it.
i see, let's keep it.
@ShubhamTaple Do you mean there’ll be UB here?
@sundb yes, using dictGetVal() when the dict type has no_value set to 1 would lead to undefined behaviour (either assert failure occurs or it would read garbage values)

I can't follow `multiple memcpy operations`, it seems that it does not correspond to this change.
why need this check `if (size > 0 && data != NULL)`?

Yep, fair point. This PR title/description is misleading: the diff only changes MemBlockWrite() (not “multiple memcpy operations”). I’ll update the wording.

As for if (size > 0 && data != NULL): it’s just a defensive guard, memcpy(dst, NULL, n) is UB when n > 0. Today, callers should always pass a real buffer, so it shouldn’t change normal behaviour, but it makes the helper safer if a future caller ever passes NULL with a non‑zero size.
IMHO, I prefer enforcing input correctness via explicit interface contracts, instead of adding defensive checks throughout core logic. Validation should live at outside.

Makes sense, I'll drop the if (size > 0 && data != NULL) guard, that kind of check belongs at the call site, not buried in the helper. I'll keep just the BLOCK_SIZE cap since that's the actual bounds fix. Would that be okay?
The upcoming C standard C2y makes `memcpy (NULL, NULL, 0)` defined (safe) behavior. I am not aware of an implementation where doing so is unsafe.

Fair point, C2y is making memcpy(NULL, NULL, 0) well-defined. My intent here was just to be conservative with today’s compilers/standards and tooling (sanitisers/static analysers), where passing NULL can still be treated as UB/flagged even if the size is 0. The guard is basically zero-cost and keeps behaviour boring across older toolchains. If you’d prefer, I can simplify it to only guard on size > 0 (and drop the data != NULL part).
@orbisai0security As this is module test code, unless you can actually make it happen, I don’t think we should go to the trouble of doing too many defences.

Makes sense, since this is just module test code and I can’t point to a real repro where data == NULL with size > 0 reaches this, I’m happy to keep it minimal.

I’ll drop the extra data != NULL guard and just keep the clearer size clamp (or I can revert the change entirely and close this PR if you prefer). Which would you like?
I prefer to close this if we can prove it.
I don't have proof of this, so I'm going to close the PR. Thanks for working through this with me.
With respect to the comment above, do we also need `RM_UnSetContextClient`?
I see in the tests that you use `NULL` for unset.
yes.  If an UnSet is more ergonomic, I'm ok with that too.  Just figured Set to NULL was ok.

i'm not certain that the priority should be like so.
maybe when `ctx->user` is explicitly set, we should prefer it?
could it be that `persistent_client->client->user` is not explicitly set and still we'll take the user from persistent_client?

perhaps we should assert/error if one tries to use module users and module clients together?

I'm not sure they should be used together, but if we do want them to be used together, I agree the order should be inverted.
if there's no specific problem this combination creates, and just that we think it's odd, let's support it (flip the priority and add a test to cover it).
done and added tests

have you considered a macro like `notifyKeyspaceEventSafe(kvobj, ...)` which could set pointer to null and call the underlying function. 

Overall, I wonder how are we going to deal with this in the long term? We are fixing a few cases currently, though, this is a potential issue in many commands. Are we going to refactor all the functions not to use pointer after KSN? or convince modules not to set keymeta in KSN callback?

* I considered a macro as you suggested, but in places with more than one KSN like hdel then del, it’s easier to use a separate macro.
* In general KSN should not modify the key. This is my best effort attempt to highlight those exceptions for the sake of RediSearch.
Why don't they fix their code then?
My concern is that we are doing something for specific commands that we know search is using. What if they need to do same thing in another command in future?

@tezc, this fix is a patch required for releasing search on disk on 8.6, which relies on the ability to write into the key metadata as part of several supported KSNs.
A more robust and generic refactor should be considered for the long term
sure, got it.

The reason it doesn't pass is that initObjectLRUOrLFU() would be called in dbAddInternal().
We can add a comment for it.

i wonder if that's not the same in many other commands (that do call it with the kvobj)?
also, the kvobj value may be used by keyModified for other things, so relying on initObjectLRUxxx might not be right.

considering the overhead of doing it again is negligible (and harmless), and considering the above, don't you think it's easier and better to just pass the value rather than a long comment?

There are still three more places over there. We can change them together. 
So now it will only be NULL if the key is deleted for keyModified().

```
    keyModified(c,c->db,c->argv[1],NULL,1);
    keyModified(c,c->db,c->argv[2],NULL,1); /* LRM already updated by dbAddInternal */
```

```
    keyModified(c,src,c->argv[1],NULL,1);
    keyModified(c,dst,c->argv[1],NULL,1); /* LRM already updated by dbAddInternal */
```

```
    /* OK! key copied. Signal modification (LRM already updated by dbAddInternal) */
    keyModified(c,dst,c->argv[2],NULL,1);
```

i'm in favor of that (only use NULL if the key is deleted).
WDYT? shall i modify these 3 places too?

> WDYT? shall i modify these 3 places too?

These three places are all called when adding key.
It means that val needs to be passed in twice and keyModified() needs to be updated twice.
Apart from this drawback, I think there is no reason not to unify them.

either `CLIENT_CLOSE_ASAP` or `c->ref_repl_buf_node != NULL` is enough, looks `c->ref_repl_buf_node != NULL` is better.
since we clear it when we close replica clients
```suggestion
    if (c->flags & CLIENT_SLAVE && c->ref_repl_buf_node != NULL &&
```
btw CLIENT_CLOSE_ASAP may not be enough in the case of `freeClientsInAsyncFreeQueue` which removes the `CLIENT_CLOSE_ASAP` flag before calling `freeClient`
If HOTKEYS section is empty, we should at least print the headline... pls take the `# Hotkeys\r\n` out of the  `if (server.hotkeys)`
